**Jicama** (pronounced *HIC-uh-muh*)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)—also called Mexican potato/turnip,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) *ahipa*, *saa got*, Chinese potato/turnip, and *lo bok*—is a tuberous root vegetable.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Jicama looks similar to a turnip[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) or a large round radish, with skin ranging from tan to brown in color.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The flesh is white,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) juicy, and crisp,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) with some comparing it to a water chestnut,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5) and it does not discolor rapidly when exposed to the air.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The flavor is fairly neutral with a touch of sweetness.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Depending on your location, jicama may be available where you purchase vegetables.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) When purchasing jicama, select tubers that are firm and unblemished with thin skin.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

Whole jicama will keep for several days at room temperature and up to several weeks in the refrigerator.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Cut-up jicama will keep in the fridge—ideally in water—for a few days.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

The skin is typically removed before eating jicama.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5) If the skin is particularly thick or tough, your best bet is to slice it off with a sharp knife, following the curve of the tuber.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Once peeled, it can be broken down as desired.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

It is a popular dietary staple in Latin America and widely grown in Mexico[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5) and Central America. It also has a presence in China and the United States.[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5) Where available, it can be eaten either raw or cooked.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:4-5) In the former case, it is often eaten in salad or as a crudité;[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) in the latter, it may be stewed or stir-fried.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) When jicama is used in cooking it tends to take on the flavors of the ingredients that it is being combined with.

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Jicama and water chestnut can sometimes be substituted for each other due to their similar textures, though the flavor is different.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)
